title: Piazza della Repubblica, Florence - A View On Cities
highlights: Piazza della Repubblica was created at the end of the nineteenth century as part of an ambitious project to  ...    Soon, however, an international campaign was launched to protest against the project, which would result in the destruction of most of the city’s historic buildings. On top of the outrage of the public opinion across Europe, the city started to run out of funds and by 1895 the redevelopment project was brought to a standstill. By that time,  ...    The most prominently visible structure on the square is the triumphal arch, known as the Arcone. It was erected in 1895 as part of the redevelopment plans. An inscription on the arch alludes to the demolition of the historic buildings: freely translated, it says ‘the old city was put out of its misery and brought back to life’. The piazza

title: Arcone Triumphal Arch at Piazza della Repubblica in Florence, Italy
highlights: the area deteriorated into a ghetto where minorities were segregated. After Florence became the capital of the Kingdom of Italy in 1865, the seediness was swept aside as medieval landmarks were demolished during a massive urbanization project. The Arcone Triumphal Arch was built in 1895 to celebrate the transformation. Its inscription reads: “The ancient center of the city restored from age-old squalor to new life.” Republic Square is now the home of  ...    Piazza della Repubblica, 50123 Firenze FI, Italy

title: Piazza della Repubblica in Florence and its Archeological Site
highlights: Florence Ancient Roman colony “Florentia” has reemerged from beneath the 18th century stone pavement in Piazza della Repubblica in Florence (Republic Square). The digging for resurfacing the square, part of the City council’s redevelopment plan, opened a window onto the square’s long history as the ancient heart of the city. Palazzo Vecchio (City Hall), in  ...    Piazza della Repubblica in Florence has always been the “square” of the city, from its establishment in Roman times to today. Here in Roman times rose the Forum of Florentia, ancient name of the city. There was a temple dedicated to the Capitoline Triad (Jupiter, Minerva, and Juno). This is where the cardo crossed the “decumanus  ...    trace of them has remained. In 1888 demolition began in the area north of the Piazza della Repubblica.
